policy_name: NIST SP 800-207 - Zero Trust Architecture Permission Policy
purpose: Apply Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) principles from NIST SP 800-207 to classify permissions. ZTA assumes no implicit trust: every permission must be minimally scoped, time-bound where possible, continuously verified, and carry compensating controls. Permissions that violate these principles are classified as Privileged or Risky.
zero_trust_tenets: All data sources and computing services are considered resources (Section 3.1)
All communication is secured regardless of network location (Section 3.2)
Access to individual enterprise resources is granted on a per-session basis (Section 3.3)
Access to resources is determined by dynamic policy (Section 3.4)
The enterprise monitors and measures the integrity and security posture of all owned and associated assets (Section 3.5)
All resource authentication and authorisation are dynamic and strictly enforced before access is allowed (Section 3.6)
The enterprise collects as much information as possible about the current state of assets, network infrastructure and communications (Section 3.7)

classification_model.privileged: Any permission that operates outside a per-session, per-resource grant — i.e., persistent standing access to control-plane operations — violates ZTA tenet 3.3 and is Privileged.
classification_model.risky: Any permission that lacks dynamic policy enforcement (no conditions, no time bounds, no context verification) violates ZTA tenet 3.4 and is Risky.

rules.name: Standing Persistent Privileged Access (No Time Bound)
rules.description: ZTA Section 3.3 requires that access be granted per-session and revoked when the session ends. A permission with no time-bound condition represents standing persistent access — the antithesis of Zero Trust. Any control-plane action without a time condition is classified as Privileged and Risky.
rules.rationale: Standing access means a principal retains elevated rights indefinitely. In ZTA, every access decision must be re-evaluated. Standing admin access is the most exploited pattern in cloud breaches.
rules.remediation: Implement Just-In-Time (JIT) access with time-bound conditions. Use AWS IAM Roles Anywhere, GCP Workload Identity, or Azure PIM to issue temporary credentials per session.

rules.name: Implicit Network-Location Trust (No IP / VPN Condition)
rules.description: ZTA Section 3.2 states that all communication is secured regardless of network location — meaning network location must not be used as the sole trust signal. However, a permission with no IP or network condition at all provides less context for the policy engine, which is Risky for sensitive actions.
rules.rationale: While ZTA does not rely solely on network location, the complete absence of network context for privileged actions means the policy engine has fewer signals to make a dynamic trust decision.
rules.remediation: Add source VPC or IP range conditions to privileged actions. Combine with MFA and device compliance conditions for a multi-signal trust evaluation.

rules.name: Broad Scope Violating Micro-Segmentation
rules.description: ZTA Section 4.2 describes micro-segmentation as a deployment model where resources are protected individually. A permission scoped to all resources ('*') or an entire account violates micro-segmentation by removing resource-level boundaries.
rules.rationale: Broad resource scope means that a compromised principal can reach all resources in the scope. Micro-segmentation limits the blast radius of a breach to a single resource or segment.
rules.remediation: Scope every permission to a specific named resource. Use resource tags, path prefixes, or resource groups as the boundary instead of account-level wildcards.

rules.name: No MFA Condition on Privileged Action
rules.description: ZTA requires continuous verification (Section 3.6). For human principals performing privileged actions, MFA is the minimum additional verification signal. A privileged permission without an MFA condition relies solely on static credential verification.
rules.nist_reference: NIST SP 800-207 Section 3.6 - All resource authentication and authorisation are dynamic
rules.rationale: Credentials (passwords, API keys) can be stolen passively. MFA ensures a second, harder-to-steal factor is verified at the time of the privileged action.
rules.remediation: Add a Condition block requiring aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent = true for all admin and IAM-management actions. Deny these actions when MFA is not present.

rules.name: Control-Plane Action by Non-Monitored Principal
rules.description: ZTA Section 3.5 requires that all assets be monitored continuously. A principal performing control-plane actions (create, modify, delete, manage permissions) must have all actions logged to a tamper-evident audit trail. The absence of enforced audit logging on a privileged principal is itself a Privileged classification trigger.
rules.nist_reference: NIST SP 800-207 Section 3.5 - Monitor and measure integrity of all assets
rules.rationale: Without enforced audit logging, privileged actions cannot be reconstructed for incident response. ZTA requires observable behaviour from every principal in every session.
rules.remediation: Enable CloudTrail / Cloud Audit Logs / K8s audit logs for all control-plane actions. Use SCP/Organisation Policy to deny disabling of audit logging. Integrate with a SIEM.

rules.name: Implicit Trust for Internal Service-to-Service Calls
rules.description: ZTA explicitly rejects the concept of a trusted internal network. A service account granted broad permissions justified solely by being 'internal' (same VPC, same account, same cluster namespace) violates the ZTA principle that network location confers no trust.
rules.nist_reference: NIST SP 800-207 Section 2.1 - ZTA rejects implicit trust for internal traffic
rules.rationale: East-west (service-to-service) traffic is the primary vector for lateral movement after initial compromise. Each service must authenticate individually regardless of colocation.
rules.remediation: Replace long-lived service account keys with Workload Identity Federation (GCP), IAM Roles for Service Accounts (AWS), or Pod Identity (Azure/K8s). Bind identity to the specific workload, not the hosting infrastructure.
